museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Dragon's Lair debuts, Lode Runner arrives, MSX is announced, the Commodore 64 price drops, and 1983 becomes both a crash year and a computer-game year at once.
museum.curatorNote: June is one of 1983's strongest exhibit months. It contains several verified dates, but regional retail still varied, so the text separates announcement, release and availability.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: June is where the year stops being one story.

items.title: MSX is announced
items.body: ASCII and Microsoft introduced the MSX standard as an attempt to unify home-computer hardware across manufacturers, especially in Japan and other non-US markets.
items.sourceNote: MSX sources list announcement on June 16, 1983.

items.title: Dragon's Lair debuts
items.body: Cinematronics and Advanced Microcomputer Systems brought Don Bluth-style animation into the arcade, creating queues, arguments and a new premium spectacle.
items.sourceNote: 1983 release summaries list Dragon's Lair on June 19, 1983.

items.title: Lode Runner is released
items.body: Doug Smith's platform-puzzle game arrived with digging, planning and a level editor, becoming a computer-game landmark rather than an arcade imitation.
items.sourceNote: Lode Runner references list original release on June 23, 1983.

items.title: Commodore cuts the C64 list price
items.body: At the June CES, Commodore lowered the C64 price to $300, intensifying the home-computer price war and pushing computers closer to console territory.
items.sourceNote: Crash and personal-computer histories cite the June 1983 C64 price cut.

items.title: The crash and the renaissance coexist
items.body: The console business was shrinking, yet Dragon's Lair, Lode Runner, EA's first wave and MSX made the medium feel anything but creatively dead.
items.sourceNote: Synthesis from June 1983 release and market anchors.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight June releases, announcements and active shelf objects.

items.title: Dragon's Lair
items.body: A laserdisc animation cabinet that turned every move into a cinematic prompt.

items.title: Lode Runner
items.body: A platform puzzle game with a construction-set spirit and durable design language.

items.title: Pssst
items.body: Ultimate's follow-up to Jetpac continued the studio's run of tight 16K Spectrum releases.
items.sourceNote: Ultimate histories list Pssst in June 1983.

items.title: Elevator Action
items.body: Taito's spy platformer used lifts, doors and timing to make a building into a playfield.
items.certainty: Japan month supported by secondary sources
items.sourceNote: Elevator Action references list June 1983 Japan and July North America.

items.title: Star Wars
items.body: Atari's vector cabinet remained a new centrepiece on arcade floors.

items.title: Jetpac
items.body: A month after release, Jetpac was already part of the Spectrum's self-image.

items.title: Archon
items.body: EA's hybrid of board strategy and action combat helped define the computer-game alternative to consoles.

items.title: M.U.L.E.
items.body: A quiet argument that computer games could be social, economic and strange.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: June's hardware case spans laserdisc, standards and price war.

items.title: Dragon's Lair cabinet
items.body: A laserdisc machine that charged premium attention and made animation itself the attraction.

items.title: MSX standard
items.body: A proposed common home-computer architecture linking Microsoft, ASCII and Japanese electronics firms.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: The June price cut made the C64 more aggressive in the home-computer fight.

items.title: Apple II and Atari 8-bit computers
items.body: Lode Runner and EA's titles show why disk-based computers became creative centres in 1983.
items.sourceNote: Lode Runner and EA first-wave context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: June's covers had unusually strong material to work with.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Laserdiscs, vectors and computer games
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for June 1983.
items.curatorNote: The June issue is remembered by later magazine retrospectives; it sits at the crossing of arcade spectacle and computer coverage.
items.body: The June issue is remembered by later magazine retrospectives; it sits at the crossing of arcade spectacle and computer coverage.
items.sourceNote: Armchair Arcade discusses Electronic Games June 1983.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Dragon's Lair and the UK cassette shelf
items.curatorNote: C&VG could place a laserdisc cabinet beside the practical world of home micro software.
items.body: C&VG could place a laserdisc cabinet beside the practical world of home micro software.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: Standards, prices and programmable play
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for June 1983.
items.curatorNote: The C64 price cut and MSX announcement belonged as much to computer buyers as to players.
items.body: The C64 price cut and MSX announcement belonged as much to computer buyers as to players.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Ultimate's early run and Spectrum listings
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for June 1983.
items.curatorNote: Jetpac and Pssst made the Spectrum feel like a machine with a software house to follow.
items.body: Jetpac and Pssst made the Spectrum feel like a machine with a software house to follow.
items.sourceNote: Ultimate release context; specific cover line not asserted.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: June was discoverable through queues and code.

items.title: Queues became publicity
items.body: Dragon's Lair lines told players something important was happening.

items.title: Editors were curators
items.body: Magazine pages decided how MSX, C64 prices and laserdiscs made sense together.

items.title: Level editors changed authorship
items.body: Lode Runner hinted that players could arrange play, not just consume it.

items.title: Standards were news before hardware
items.body: MSX reached many readers first as a promise on paper.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: June felt contradictory in the best possible way.

items.title: The arcade became a cinema booth
items.body: Dragon's Lair looked like an impossible leap.

items.title: The computer became a workshop
items.body: Lode Runner made design feel playable.

items.title: Cheap power changed the room
items.body: A C64 price cut made a serious games computer easier to imagine.

items.title: The crash stopped being the only story
items.body: The industry was wounded, but not quiet.
